title: ExoMol line lists -- LIX. High-temperature line list for N2O
journal: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
abstract: New hot line lists for five isotopologues of N2O are presented, for the parent 14 16 and 4 singly substituted species 14 17 , 14 18 , 14 15 16 and 15 14 16 . The line lists have been computed with the variational program TROVE using a new empirical potential energy surface (PES) and an accurate ab initio dipole moment surface of N2O Ames-1. The PES was obtained by fitting to experimentally derived energies of N2O compiled using the well established MARVEL procedure. Here we also introduce an `artificial symmetry group' for an efficient construction of rotation-vibrational basis set of a linear non-symmetric triatomic molecule of the XYZ type. The line lists cover the rotational excitations up to and the wavenumber range up to 20000 cm-1. MARVEL energies are also used to improve predicted line positions resulting in excellent agreement with the available experimental spectra, as demonstrated. An extensive comparison with existing line lists for N2O HITRAN, HITEMP, NOSL-296, NOSD-1000 and Ames-296K is provided. The line lists are freely accessible from www.exomol.com www.exomol.com .
body_text:
Introduction

N\2O (nitrous oxide) is a trace atmospheric species on Earth with notable spectral features and a dominantly biological origin. The emission of the 4.5 m band of N\2O in the Earth atmosphere is known to be non-local thermodynamic equilibrium (non-LTE) . N\2O is therefore one of the molecules suggested as an observable bio-signature in Earth-like exoplanets . Its possible detection in exoplanets features strongly in telescope proposals and atmospheric models . Due to its atmospheric importance, there have been a wealth of experimental and theoretical studies on N\2O, see for a detailed review of the spectroscopic literature. N\2O has been detected in the interstellar medium , but as yet not in exoplanets.

Owing to the importance of N\2O for atmospheric studies, a number of nitrous oxide line lists for exist in the spectroscopic data bases. HITRAN 2020 provides an accurate spectroscopic data sets for multiple isotopologues of N\2O for (ambient temperature) terrestrial and planetary atmospheric applications based on the wealth of experimental data and targeting experimental accuracy. Its hot-temperature partner HITEMP provides hot line lists for N\2O isotopologues intended for high temperature applications. HITEMP line lists aim to retain the accuracy of the lines in HITRAN by providing the completeness required at high temperatures. Another well-known N\2O line list family is produced and maintained by the laboratories in Tomsk, a high-accuracy, low-temperature line list NOSL-296 and its high-temperature partner NOSD-1000 . All four line lists are empirical in nature produced using effective Hamiltonian and dipole moment models. They typically provide line positions, line intensities ( K or 1000 K), Einstein coefficients, lower state energies, line broadening parameters (except NOSL-296) and line shifts (except NOSD-1000 and NOSL-296) parameters and are equipped with associated quantum numbers.

Recently, produced room temperature line lists for N\2O isotopologues Ames-296K using an accurate empirical potential energy surface (PES) Ames-1 and a high level \ dipole moment surface (DMS) Ames-1. This line list is different from the effective models as it is based on the so-called "Best Theory + Reliable High-Resolution experiment (BTRHE)" strategy , where the variational methods are used to solve the nuclear motion Schr\" o dinger equation. The empirical potential energy surfaces (PESs) are obtained by fitting to experimental energies/line positions and
high level \ dipole moment surfaces (DMS) are used in intensity calculations.

In this work we follow a similar variational methodology and report empirical line lists for five isotopologues of N2O, including the parent 14 16 and four singly substituted species 14 17 , 14 18 , 14 15 16 and 15 14 16 , valid up to 2000 K. The line lists cover the wavenumber range up to 20000 cm-1 and rotational excitations up to . The variational program \ is used to solve the Schr\" o dinger equation for the nuclear motion of N\2O and to compute the ro-vibrational energies and wavefunctions. For the intensities a high level \ DMS reported by is used. The production of the line lists is heavily based on the recently reported experimentally energies of 14 16 derived using the MARVEL (measured active rotation vibration energy levels) procedure of ; these empirical energy levels are used to refine an \ potential energy surface (PES) of . The line list for the parent isotopologue is improved by substituting the calculated ro-vibrational energies with experimentally derived values by where available.

Variational nuclear motion calculations

For the variational calculations, we used an exact kinetic energy operator (KEO) with a bisector frame as implemented in \ in combination with the associated Laguerre polynomials as reported by . The \ computational protocol consists of four steps, described in detail below. Nuclear masses were used: 13.999233945 Da (14N) and 14.99626884 Da (15N); 15.990525980 (16O), 16.99474312 Da (17O) and 17.99477097 Da (18O).

Step 1. The primitive vibrational basis set is comprised of three types of one-dimensional basis functions. For the stretching modes N-O and N-N, two 1D vibrational Schr\"odinger equations were solved using the Numerov-Cooley method for 1D Hamiltonians constructed by freezing two other vibrational modes except the one in question. For the N-N and N-O stretching basis functions and , respectively, grids of 2000 and 3000 points were used, ranging between \ and \ around the corresponding equilibrium values 1.1282021 and 1.1845554 , respectively. For the bending mode N-N-O basis set , a similar 1D Hamiltonian was constructed and variationally solved using the associated Laguerre polynomials on a grid of 12000 points of , where is the vibrational angular momentum index

defined as and was set to 120 .

The (exact) kinetic energy operator is constructed numerically as a formal expansion in terms of the inverse powers of the stretching coordinates ( ): and around a non-rigid configuration defined by the points on the grid. The singularities of the kinetic energy operator at ( and ) are resolved analytically with the help of the factors in the definition of the associated Laguerre basis set. The details of the KEO and the matrix elements involved are given by .

Step 2. The individual 1D vibrational basis functions are then assigned to an irreducible representation (irrep) for the symmetry group chosen. Here we implemented and used the so-called artificial symmetry group with a procedure analogous to the one described by but for the case of a non-symmetric molecule.

The artificial symmetry group consists of one-dimensional, real irreps, each of which is correlated with the vibrational index .
From we select two elements and match each with a \ element. 

The irreps of are labelled as and irreps with an extra subscript

(see Table ), e.g., . For example, a vibrational function with and transforming as in \ would be assigned the symmetry in the . The 0-superscripted irreps are the only physical irreps, matched to and of \ together with the corresponding characters of each element, while all irreps of are non-physical, i.e. "artificial". The full description of this case is given below, for the first time.

A 3D vibrational ( -dependent) basis set for the Hamiltonian is formed for each as symmetry adapted products given by:
 
where is the vibrational symmetry in and will be that of the . This is because the function as the irreps of and are , so have no impact of the irrep of the combined vibrational function. Thus, the symmetry of the vibrational basis function is wholly classified by the corresponding value of . The total vibrational basis set is the subject to the cutoff based on the following truncation scheme,
 

The vibrational eigen-solutions are found for a range of values of by solving for the Hamiltonian , for all corresponding irreps or covering available for , where we used .

 

Step 3.
The final ro-vibrational basis set for calculations is then formed as a contracted, symmetrised product of the vibrational functions:
 
where the rotational part is a symmetrised combination of the rigid rotor functions and the rotational quantum number ( ) is constrained to the vibrational parameter ( ) with the the rotational irrep defined as
 
where is the parity of the Wang-type rotational basis function .
Here , and are the total, vibrational and rotational symmetries in , , and is the projection of the angular momentum on the molecular and laboratory axes, respectively, and is a TROVE vibrational index to count the functions regardless of their symmetry, see .

When these are combined, the symmetry of the resultant function is 0-subscripted only if the subscripts of the bending and rotational functions are the same. If not, then the subscript is not 0. Moreover, the symmetry of the combined function corresponds to the \ symmetry - in the sense established above - and thus, ignoring the subscript, is the same symmetry had we worked solely in the \ group. For example, is while is .

Step 4: Intensity calculations. The variational wavefunctions computed at Step 3 are then used to compute ro-vibrational line strengths of N\2O for transitions up to between all states satisfying the electric dipole selections rules
 
Here and have the standard correlation rules with the spectroscopic parities and as follows: the parity of and , 1 and , respectively, correspond to for and for . The lower and upper states were limited by the energy thresholds of 20000 \ and 36000 , respectively. The range of transition wavenumbers is 0 to 20000 . The artificial symmetry states and ( ) must be excluded from the intensity calculations. In , this can be done by setting the corresponding statistical weight factors to zero, similar to how the non-physical states were excluded in the HOOH calculations using the extended symmetry group 2(EM) by or by simply not computing these states on the first place. The nuclear statistical weights of the physical states of the isotopologues considered are listed in Table . A detailed description of the procedure to construct the irreps is given in Appendix .

The main advantage of is that it permits us to use the efficient machinery of irreducible representations implemented in \ to construct the -dependent vibrational basis and also to combine it with the rotational -dependent basis functions. In this way, the non-standard basis set (for ) is exploited by the same generalised \ implementation (with only some minimal changes) used for any other, more standard basis sets. This is in contrast to an overhaul of the program which may otherwise have been required.

Quantum numbers

The computed \ ro-vibrational energies are assigned by the two rigorous quantum `numbers', , the rotational angular momentum quantum number, and the total symmetry in and also identified by the eigen-state counting number (in the order of increasing energies).
 In order to help in spectroscopic applications, we also use approximate quantum numbers (QN) associated with the corresponding largest basis set contributions . This provides a measure for how close the variationally computed wavefunctions are to the rotational and vibrational basis set used. The vibrational \ QNs are associated with the primitive basis set excitation numbers and (see the description of the vibrational basis set above). Remember that the rotational quantum number is constrained to the value of . These `local' mode quantum numbers are approximately correlated to the spectroscopic, normal mode quantum numbers , , of N\2O as follows:
 
with defined the same way in both conventions.
According to the normal mode convention, also used by HITRAN, and are two stretching quantum numbers associated with the NO and NN modes, respectively; is the linear molecule bending quantum number; and are two bending quantum numbers satisfying the standard condition on the vibrational angular momentum of an isotropic 2D Harmonic oscillator 
 
Following recent spectroscopic recommendations for the quantum numbers of nitrous oxide , we also add the polyad quantum number scheme, , with the polyad number given by
 
and is the polyad counting number within the same , and . This scheme has been shown to be more practical for description of nitrous oxide spectra characterised with
many resonances among the excited vibrational states.

Potential energy surface and empirical refinement

The PES of N\2O was originally taken from the \ study of , which we then converted to the Morse type expansion as the form more comfortable for . We also made sure the expansion point was taken exactly at the minimum.
The PES was expressed analytically by an expansion
 
in terms of
 
Here, and are the bond lengths and is the bond angle.
In Eq. , are the expansion parameters with maximum expansion order with the linear expansion parameters fixed to zero and
 
is to ensure that the PES behaves correctly when the distance between the outer N and O atoms,
 
becomes small. The parameters and were set to numerical values taken from (see the expansion parameter list of the PES in the supplementary material).

The ` ' expansion parameters are then refined by fitting to the N\2O MARVEL energies for , 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 8, 10, 15, 18, 20, 23, 25, 28, 30, 33, 35, 38, 40, 43, 45, 50, 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80 (6563 in total). The PES was constrained to the \ PES of in the fitting - in order ensure the refined PES maintained a realistic shape. The quality of the fit is demonstrated in Fig. , where the fitting residuals are plotted as the obs.-calc. differences (in cm-1) between the MARVEL and computed TROVE N\2O term values. The total root-mean-square (RMS) error for all 17532 MARVEL energies ranging from 0 to is 0.02 \ which can be broken down to 0.003, 0.007, 0.01, 0.01, 0.025, 0.06 and 0.04 \ for 7 consecutive 2000 \ energy windows, 0-2000 , 2000-4000 , , 12000-14000 , respectively, as shown in detail in Fig. . A full list of the observed (MARVEL) versus calculated ( ) energies is given as supplementary material

A Fortran 95 subroutine of the PES with the associated potential parameters is also provided as part of in the supporting information.

 

Dipole moment surfaces and uncertainties of intensities

In the line list production, the intensity were computed using the most recent, high-level of theory (CCSD(T)/aug-cc-pV(T,Q,5)Z extrapolated to one electron basis set limit) \ DMS of . It has been demonstrated to provide high-quality intensities of N\2O isotopologues. This DMS is analytically represented using the pseudo-charge form via its projections to the molecular bonds and therefore needs to be rotated the bisector frame used in \ ro-vibrational calculations. This is done on the fly at each bending grid point by re-expanding it in terms of the stretching displacement and around the equilibrium geometry (see above) in the polynomials of the 12th order using the finite differences and employing the quadrupole-precision (see ).

The N\2O TYM\ line list

Line list structure

Using the methodology described, line lists, called TYM, for five isotopologues of N\2O were computed. They cover the 0 to 20000 \ range for ro-vibrational states with rotational excitation up to . The lower and upper state energy thresholds were chosen to be 10000 and 28000 , respectively. The parent istopologue is represented by 1 532 806 222 transitions between 2 078 676 states. The transitions are divided into 1000 \ wavenumber ranges to make them more manageable. Table lists numbers of states and transitions in line lists of five N\2O isotopologues as well as the nuclear-spin degeneracy ; note our partition functions include the full nuclear spin degeneracy.

 

The line lists is provided in the ExoMol data format . An extract from one of the .trans transition files of 14 16 is shown in Table . They contain upper and lower state ID numbers along with the Einstein A coefficient (in ) of the transition between the states. An extract from the .states states file of 14 16 is given in Table , respectively; it contains a list of the ro-vibrational states with state ID numbers, energies (in ), uncertainties (in ), state lifetimes and quantum numbers. We use the standard spectroscopic normal mode quantum numbers in the .states file but also keep the TROVE quantum numbers for traceability. The mapping between the two sets is discussed in Sec. . In contrast to the rigorous quantum numbers and (irrep), the non-rigorous quantum numbers and even are approximate and are defined using the largest eigen-contribution approach. As a note of warning, this procedure cannot guarantee comprehensive quantum number descriptions with unique and unambiguous labels thus serving more as a measure of the main character of the state in question.

The .states file for 14 16 is also "MARVELised", i.e. the \ energies are replaced with the empirical (MARVEL) energies from where available. The uncertainties are defined either as the MARVEL uncertainty if available, or for calculated levels estimated as (in cm-1):
 
where - are the \ normal mode quantum numbers.
These uncertainties are only approximate and designed to grow steadily with increasing rotational and vibrational excitation, where
we tend to be conservative in estimating values for highly excited states.

In the case of the minor isotopologues, we could not establish a solid correlation between the spectroscopic normal mode and 's local mode quantum numbers. We therefore retain only a few quantum numbers in their .states files as illustrated in Table : the polyad number , vibrational angular momentum , a polyad counting number as well as , and . The polyad number is estimated using the following simplified relation
 
where is the energy term value of state , is a rough estimate of the polyad quanta and is the corresponding vibrational angular momentum.

 

 

 

Partition functions for all five isotopologues of N\2O were computed on a grid of 1 K from 0 to 2000 K and are available as .pf files from the ExoMol website. For 14 16 , is illustrated in Fig. , where we also show the TIPS partition function from HITRAN 2020 , which is essentially identical to that reported in . Our partition function is larger indicating better completeness at higher temperatures.

Temperature- and pressure-dependent molecular opacities of 14 16 based on the TYM\ line list have been generated using the ExoMolOP procedure for four exoplanet atmospheric retrieval codes: ARCiS , TauREx , NEMESIS and petitRADTRANS and available on the respective line list page of the species in question.

The \ input files are provided, with spectroscopic model used in TROVE calculations in the form of a TROVE input file, containing the potential energy and dipole moment surface parameters as well as the basis set specifications. This input file can be used with the Fortran code \ freely available from GitHub via www.github.org/exomol www.github.org/exomol https://spectrove.readthedocs.io .

 

Zero-pressure cross-sections of 14 16 are provided via the ExoMol cross-sections app. It can generate absorption cross-sections with the Doppler broadening line profile for temperatures from 100 to 2000 K covering the wavenumber range from 0 to 20000 \ on a grid with a resolution of up to 0.01 .

Spectra simulations

Figure provides an overview of the spectrum of 14 16 at three temperatures, K, K and 2000 K from 0 to 20000 , computed using the TYM\ line list. It is worth noting the profound temperature dependence of the band shapes in IR. In particular, the visual centres of the vibrational bands

at K appear significantly red shifted comparing to the room temperature spectrum, which is highlighted in the inset of this figure showing the 1800 - 5000 \ window.

 

In Figures and we present comparisons of TYM\ with the nitrous oxide line lists from other spectroscopic data bases. Figure compares room temperature ( K) absorption spectra of 14 16 generated using the N\2O line lists from Ames-296K , NOSL-296 and N\2O HITRAN 2020 at low and high resolutions. A more extensive and detailed comparison of high-resolution spectra generated using these four line lists is given in Appendix .
There is a generally good agreement of ExoMol TYM\ with all three databases, all the way up to the NIR region, where HITRAN' N\2O is absent, although there are also some differences in the weaker bands. The intensities of TYM\ are more similar to Ames-296K, which is expected due to the same dipole moment Ames-1 used in our work. The deviation from the NOSL-296 line list grows towards the NIR, where it is increasingly incomplete, especially above 10000 , while Ames-296K only covers up to 15000 (see Appendix ).

It should be noted, that the TYM\ line positions are shown before applying the MARVELisation procedure, i.e. they are based on the original quality of the TYM\ spectroscopic model. This is important as it lends some trust to the quality of the predicted TYM\ spectra, which will need to be tested with the future experimental data.

Figure offers a similar comparison of TYM\ with hot line lists for N\2O, HITEMP and NOSD-1000 , where their absorption spectra of 14 16 at K are shown. Again, there is a generally good agreement between all three line lists in the IR and lower, except for some cases of weak intensities as illustrated in the right display of this figure around 4800 . The main disagreement is attributed to incompleteness of HITEMP and NOSD-1000 starting from NIR.

Figure illustrates the spectroscopic coverage of the MARVELised data in a TYM\ spectrum of 14 12 at K and compares it to that of HITRAN and Ames-296K . HITRAN 2020 contains 33265 lines below 7800 , our line list produces 365 041 MARVELised lines, i.e. with experimentally accurate line positions, for K and with intensities below cm/molecule.

 

 

 

Figure illustrates the relative importance of the spectra of the isotopologues of N\2O at room temperature in an IR spectroscopic window, where the individual spectra are scaled by their natural abundances taken from HITRAN. The minor isotopologues provide substantial contribution the overall spectrum of N\2O with intensities significantly larger than the HITRAN cutoff of cm/molecule. Another illustration of the possible detectability of N\2O is presented in Fig. , where we overlay the absorption spectrum of 14 12 at K with absorption spectra of other atmospheric triatomic molecules, SO\2, CO\2, HDO and H\2O. The strongest and most prominent absorption feature of N\2O, which is also in a water window, is at 4.5 m, which is in a close vicinity from the 4.3 m band of CO\2.

 

 

On the uncertainty of the calculated intensities

It is now a well-recognised problem that numerical noise associated with variational calculations can cause an overestimation of intensities of high-overtone transitions which has been discussed in a series of publications on diatomic molecules by Medvedev et al., see, e.g. .
Although there is no obvious indication of such plateaus forming at higher frequencies in our N\2O spectra in Figs - - they seem to show a nice exponential decrease of the intensities of the overtone bands, except perhaps the region of around 18000-20000 - we decided to perform a more quantitative test of our intensities adapting the approach of suggested by . To this end, we have computed produced a line list for N\2O using a different \ DMS by .
The result of this test is illustrated in Fig. (left display), where we show K cross sections of 14 16 computed using two \ DMSs, `Ames-1' and `Schr\" o der et al. (2015)' in conjunctions with our refined PES. The intensities generally agree up to about 13000 , but less so above 13000 . In fact, the `DMS Schr\" o der et al. (2015)' intensities appear to have a more natural exponential decline above 16000 , where the `DMS Ames-1' intensities have a slight increase of the `Ames-1 intensities over `DMS Schr\" o der et al. (2015)' indicating a possible formation of a high-overtone plateau and could be a result of the deviation from the so-called Normal Intensity Distribution Law (NIDL) . The analytical DMS representation of has fewer parameters than that of (39 versus 572) and is presumably more stable for numerical errors at high overtones.

As a high resolution illustration, in the right display of Fig. , the corresponding K line intensities of the bands are shown, which we also compare to the HITRAN 2020 values. The `DMS Schr\" o der et al. (2015)' intensities (i.e. computed using our model and DMS by ) appear to deviate from the HITRAN values up to about 34\%. Analogous deviations were also found for the excited bending bands. Our choice to use the more recent DMS Ames-1 for the ExoMol line lists productions was influenced by this higher quality at lower energies.

 

Conclusion

In this work, ro-vibrational line lists for five main isotopologues of N\2O are presented. The line lists were computed using the variational approach \ employing a new empirical PES and an \ DMS from . The PES was generated via a fit to the experimentally derived energy term values of 14 16 from the MARVEL set by covering the rotational excitations up to . These energies were then used to improve the calculated values of 14 16 in the TYM\ line list. The line lists cover an extended wavenumber range up to \ ( m) and the rotational excitations up to with the estimated temperature coverage up to K. Owing to the MARVELisation procedure used, we were able to predict over 300 000 new transitions of 14 16 with experimental accuracy. The line lists contain over a billion transitions each, ranging from 1.3 to 1.7 billion, depending on the isotopologue. At present only
the parent 14 16 line list has been MARVELised; however, there are also extensive spectroscopic data available
for the other isotopologues which are currently subject to a MARVEL analysis. Updated line lists using these results
will be made available in due course.

In calculations, an artificial symmetry group ( ) was derived and used in \ calculations to facilitate the basis set construction.

Data Availability

The data underlying this article are available as part of the supporting information from the ExoMol database at http://www.exomol.com www.exomol.com .
The TYM\ line list (states, transition, partition function files and a TROVE input specifying the spectroscopic model of N\2O) and opacities can be downloaded from www.exomol.com www.exomol.com .
The refined PES of N\2O TYM used in this work is provided as part of the supplementary material, together with a comparison of the calculated TYM energies of 14 16 with the experimentally derived (MARVEL) energies .

Supporting Information

The TROVE input files are given as a supplementary materials to this article.

 

Appendix: Artificial symmetry

The internal vibrational modes and used in our model are effectively coordinates of a bent molecule. These span two irreducible representations, and (or and ) of (M) (isomorphic to C ) and do not reflect the symmetry properties of a linear molecule. An alternative symmetry description of vibrational and rotational functions of a linear molecule is offered by the symmetry group (EM) spanning much more versatile irreducible representations with , , , , \ for 0, 1, 2, 3, and 0, 1, 2, 3, for the vibrations and rotations respectively. Their advantage of this description is this direct and unique association of the indices and with irreducible representations of (EM), with all the efficient and useful properties. Here we introduce the artificial symmetry group associated with the basis set of a bent molecule but possessing this property of the linear molecule group (EM) where the corresponding irreps are uniquely classified by indices or .

In order construct the irreducible representations of the finite artificial extended molecular symmetry group , we follow the n(AEM) methodology by developed for symmetric linear molecules. The group in question is thus defined by
 
where Z 2 is the cyclic group of order 2 and, therefore, it consists of the set with addition modulo 2.
The integer depends on the value of and is given by
 
where rounds up and is the maximum value of (or ). For example, the character table of is given in Table . This corresponds to and the number of irreps in the group equals the number necessary for the reassignment of the bending and rotational functions, though this is not true in general because of the rounding applied in Eq. . In either case, the extra irreps are needed as seen below. 

When combining bending function that transform as of with rotational functions which transform as , their product should transform as for some if . If , then they should transform as . For example, should be .

The symmetry group for a general is implemented into \ where the effects of the group operations on the coordinates is as follows: all operations leave the vibrational coordinates invariant; the operations (in the notation of Table ) leave the rotational functions invariant while the operation has the same effect as the operation.

Left display: a log-scale illustration of the coverage of TYM, Ames-296K and NOSL-296 using a Gaussian line profile with HWHM of 1 \ was used. HITRAN line intensity values are indicated by red empty circles (in the upper and lower displays), NOSL-296 values are with dark blue circles (bottom displays), Ames-296K lines are given by green sticks (bottom display) and TYM\ lines are shown by blue sticks in the upper displays.
